a: Software architecture is the high-level plan for how a system is split into parts and how those parts work together. It is the big-picture shape (which pieces exist, what each does, how they talk) rather than the detailed code inside any one piece.
fs: Architecture is the set of important, hard-to-change decisions about a system's structure: its parts, their responsibilities, and how they interact.
fj: Thinking architecture is just drawing boxes, or that it means the actual code. It is the structural decisions that shape the code, not the code itself.

a: A component is one self-contained part of a system that does a specific job, like a login service or a payment module. You build a system by combining many components, each handling its own piece.
fs: A component is a unit with a clear responsibility and a defined boundary, replaceable without rewriting the rest of the system.
fj: Confusing a component with a single function or file. A component is a whole capability, often many files, exposed through one boundary.

a: Coupling is how much one part of a system depends on another. Loose coupling means parts barely rely on each other, so you can change one without breaking the others; tight coupling means a change in one forces changes elsewhere.
fs: Coupling measures inter-part dependency; aim for loose coupling so components change independently behind stable interfaces.
fj: Assuming less coupling is always better at any cost. The goal is loose, not zero; parts still need to cooperate through clear interfaces.

a: Layered architecture stacks a system into horizontal layers, each with one role, like a presentation layer (UI), a business-logic layer, and a data layer. Each layer only talks to the one directly below it, which keeps responsibilities separate and easy to follow.
fs: A layered design separates concerns into stacked tiers where each layer depends only on the layer beneath, so logic, UI, and storage stay decoupled.
fj: Thinking layers are separate computers. Layers are a logical split of code by responsibility; they often run together in one app.

q: What is the difference between a monolith and microservices?
a: A monolith puts the whole application in one single deployable unit, while microservices split it into many small services that run and deploy separately and talk over the network. A monolith is simpler to start; microservices let teams scale and ship parts independently.
fs: Monolith = one deployable; microservices = many independently deployable services. The trade is simplicity versus independent scaling and deployment.
fj: Thinking microservices are always the better choice. They add network and operational complexity; a monolith is often the right call early on.
